chunks.text: # Water Purification and Procurement *Original content based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles (public domain)* ## Water Procurement The human body can survive weeks without food but only days without water. In survival situations, finding and purifying water is your highest priority after immediate shelter and medical needs. ### Finding Water Sources **Natural Sources:** - Streams and rivers (preferably moving water) - Lakes and ponds (still water requires more purification) - Springs (generally safer than surface water) - Rain collection (cleanest natural source) - Dew collection (use absorbent cloth on grass at dawn) - Snow and ice (melt before consuming, never eat directly) **Environmental Indicators:** - Follow animal tracks (often lead to water) - Observe bird flight patterns at dawn and dusk - Look for green vegetation in otherwise dry areas - Check low-lying areas and val

metadata.title: Water Purification and Procurement
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: bird flight patterns at dawn and dusk - Look for green vegetation in otherwise dry areas - Check low-lying areas and valley bottoms - Listen for sounds of flowing water **WARNING:** Avoid water with: - Soapy appearance or petroleum smell - Algae blooms (blue-green discoloration) - Dead animals nearby - Chemical odors - Brackish or salty taste ## Purification Methods All water from natural sources should be considered contaminated and must be purified before drinking. ### 1. Boiling (Most Reliable) **Procedure:** 1. Bring water to a rolling boil 2. Maintain boil for 1 minute at sea level 3. Add 1 additional minute for every 1,000 feet above sea level 4. Allow to cool before drinking **Effectiveness:** Kills all bacteria, viruses, and parasites. Does not remove chemical contaminants. **Advantage:** No special equipment needed, 100% effective against biological threats. **Disadvantage:** Re

metadata.title: Water Purification and Procurement
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: ontaminants. **Advantage:** No special equipment needed, 100% effective against biological threats. **Disadvantage:** Requires fuel and container, does not improve taste. ### 2. Chemical Purification **Iodine Tablets:** - Add 2 tablets per liter of clear water - Double dosage for cloudy water - Wait 30 minutes before drinking (60 minutes if water is very cold) - Shelf life: 3-5 years if properly stored **Chlorine Bleach (Unscented, 5-6% sodium hypochlorite):** - Add 2 drops per liter of clear water - Double dosage for cloudy water - Wait 30 minutes, should have slight chlorine smell - If no smell, repeat dosage and wait another 15 minutes **WARNING:** Do not use iodine for more than 2-3 weeks continuously. Not recommended for pregnant women or those with thyroid conditions. ### 3. Solar Still **Construction:** 1. Dig hole approximately 3 feet wide and 2 feet deep 2. Place collection cont

metadata.title: Water Purification and Procurement
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: ons. ### 3. Solar Still **Construction:** 1. Dig hole approximately 3 feet wide and 2 feet deep 2. Place collection container in center 3. Cover hole with clear plastic sheet 4. Seal edges with soil and rocks 5. Place small rock in center to create cone shape directing condensation to container 6. Can add vegetation or urine around (not in) container to increase moisture **Yield:** 1-3 pints per day depending on conditions. **Best Use:** Desert or arid environments with intense sun. ### 4. Filtration **Improvised Filter:** 1. Use cloth, sand, charcoal, and gravel in layers 2. Pour water through multiple times 3. **CRITICAL:** Filtration alone does NOT purify water 4. Must combine with boiling or chemical treatment **Commercial Filters:** - Look for filters rated to 0.2 microns or smaller - Follow manufacturer instructions for flow rate and lifespan - Many filters do NOT remove viruses (t

metadata.title: Water Purification and Procurement
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: microns or smaller - Follow manufacturer instructions for flow rate and lifespan - Many filters do NOT remove viruses (too small) - Combine with chemical treatment in areas with known viral contamination ### 5. UV Purification **Method:** - Fill clear plastic bottle - Place in direct sunlight for 6 hours (or 2 days if cloudy) - UV radiation kills most pathogens **Limitations:** Only works with clear water in clear containers. Not effective against all parasites. ## Signs of Contaminated Water **Visual Indicators:** - Cloudy or murky appearance - Surface film or oil sheen - Foam (except natural waterfall foam) - Unusual colors (red, green, brown) **Smell Indicators:** - Sulfur or "rotten egg" smell - Chemical or petroleum odor - Strong decay smell **Environmental Indicators:** - Dead fish or animals in or near water - Absence of any aquatic life or vegetation - Mining or industrial activi

metadata.title: Water Purification and Procurement
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: ors:** - Dead fish or animals in or near water - Absence of any aquatic life or vegetation - Mining or industrial activity upstream - Agricultural runoff (fertilizer, pesticides) ## Water Storage **Short-term (1-3 days):** - Use clean, food-grade containers - Keep covered and in shade - Consume oldest water first **Long-term (weeks to months):** - Sterilize containers with boiling water first - Add 2 drops of unscented bleach per liter - Store in cool, dark location - Rotate supply every 6 months - Mark containers with purification date ## Daily Water Requirements **Minimum Survival:** 2 liters per day **Active/Hot Climate:** 4-6 liters per day **Extreme Exertion:** Up to 10 liters per day **Dehydration Signs:** - Dark yellow urine - Decreased urination - Dry mouth and lips - Dizziness or lightheadedness - Rapid heartbeat - Confusion or irritability **Action:** Increase water intake imme

metadata.title: Water Purification and Procurement
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: lips - Dizziness or lightheadedness - Rapid heartbeat - Confusion or irritability **Action:** Increase water intake immediately. If dehydration is severe, add pinch of salt and sugar to water to help absorption. ## Emergency Water Ration If water is scarce: - Reduce activity during hottest parts of day - Breathe through nose, not mouth - Do not eat if you have no water (digestion requires water) - Never drink seawater, urine, or blood (accelerates dehydration) - Avoid alcohol and caffeine Remember: It's better to drink questionable water than to become severely dehydrated. If you must choose, drink first and purify later if immediate need is critical. Dehydration kills faster than waterborne illness.

metadata.title: Water Purification and Procurement
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: # Fire Starting Techniques *Original content based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles (public domain)* ## Importance of Fire Fire provides warmth, water purification, cooking, signaling, predator deterrent, and psychological comfort. Mastering multiple fire-starting methods is essential for survival. ## Fire Requirements **The Fire Triangle:** 1. **Heat** — ignition source 2. **Fuel** — material that burns 3. **Oxygen** — air flow Remove any element and fire fails. All three must be balanced. ## Fuel Preparation **Tinder** (catches spark easily): - Dry grass, leaves, or moss - Birch bark (peels easily, burns even when wet) - Cotton balls, lint, or char cloth - Fine wood shavings - Pine needles - Fatwood or resinous wood **Kindling** (small sticks, finger-width): - Dead twigs still on trees (drier than ground wood) - Pencil-to-thumb thickness - Split wood from larger pieces **Fuel

metadata.title: Fire Starting Techniques
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: - Dead twigs still on trees (drier than ground wood) - Pencil-to-thumb thickness - Split wood from larger pieces **Fuel Wood** (sustained burning): - Arm-thickness and larger - Dead standing wood is driest - Split wood burns better than rounds - Hardwoods burn longer than softwoods **Test for Dryness:** Dead wood should snap cleanly, not bend. ## Fire Starting Methods ### 1. Friction Methods **Bow Drill:** *Components needed:* - Bow (curved branch with cordage) - Drill (straight stick, 6-8" long, 3/4" diameter) - Fireboard (flat softwood piece) - Handhold (protects top hand) - Tinder bundle *Procedure:* 1. Cut V-notch in fireboard where drill will spin 2. Place tinder bundle under notch 3. Loop bow string around drill once 4. Place drill in notch, hold handhold on top 5. Bow back and forth rapidly with steady downward pressure 6. Smoke will appear, then glowing ember in notch 7. Carefull

metadata.title: Fire Starting Techniques
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: . Bow back and forth rapidly with steady downward pressure 6. Smoke will appear, then glowing ember in notch 7. Carefully transfer ember to tinder bundle 8. Blow gently to create flame **Efficiency:** High effort, but reliable with practice. **Hand Drill** (similar but uses hands instead of bow): - More difficult, requires faster spinning - Best with very dry softwood and optimal tinder ### 2. Flint and Steel / Ferro Rod **Procedure:** 1. Prepare tinder bundle first 2. Hold striker at 45° angle 3. Strike down length of ferro rod with force 4. Direct sparks into tinder 5. Blow gently when smoke appears **Tips:** - Strike the steel/striker, not the rod - Char cloth catches sparks more easily - Keep rod dry and scrape oxidation off before use **Advantage:** Works in all weather, nearly unlimited uses. ### 3. Lens Method **Using Magnifying Glass or Clear Water:** *Procedure:* 1. Create tinde

metadata.title: Fire Starting Techniques
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: ather, nearly unlimited uses. ### 3. Lens Method **Using Magnifying Glass or Clear Water:** *Procedure:* 1. Create tinder bundle with finest material possible 2. Focus sunlight to smallest, brightest point on tinder 3. Hold steady (30 seconds to 2 minutes) 4. Smoke will appear, then ember 5. Blow gently to create flame **Improvised Lenses:** - Bottom of clear plastic or glass bottle filled with water - Ice shaped into lens (smooth and clear) - Eyeglasses or camera lens **Limitation:** Requires direct sunlight, doesn't work at night or in overcast conditions. ### 4. Battery and Steel Wool **Procedure:** 1. Stretch steel wool to thin strand 2. Touch both battery terminals to steel wool simultaneously 3. Steel wool ignites immediately 4. Transfer to tinder bundle quickly **Works with:** 9V battery, AA battery (touch terminals together with steel wool bridge), car battery. **WARNING:** Produ

metadata.title: Fire Starting Techniques
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: *Works with:** 9V battery, AA battery (touch terminals together with steel wool bridge), car battery. **WARNING:** Produces very hot, fast flame. Have tinder ready before starting. ### 5. Matches and Lighters **Best Practices:** - Store in waterproof container - Strike-anywhere matches are most versatile - Lighters fail in extreme cold (warm in hands first) - Always carry backup method ## Fire Types **Tepee Fire:** - Best for: Quick heat, boiling water - Arrangement: Tinder in center, kindling/fuel in cone shape - Burns hot and fast **Log Cabin Fire:** - Best for: Cooking, sustained heat - Arrangement: Square layers of progressively smaller wood - Burns evenly and long **Star Fire:** - Best for: Fuel conservation - Arrangement: Logs radiate out from center like star points - Push logs inward as they burn **Signal Fire:** - Add green vegetation or wet materials to create thick white smoke

metadata.title: Fire Starting Techniques
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: nts - Push logs inward as they burn **Signal Fire:** - Add green vegetation or wet materials to create thick white smoke - Build large to be visible from distance - Prepare three fires in triangle pattern (international distress signal) ## Fire Safety **Site Selection:** - Clear 10-foot diameter down to bare soil - Away from overhanging branches - Sheltered from wind but with ventilation - Never inside enclosed shelter without proper chimney **Weather Considerations:** - In wind: dig fire pit or build windbreak - In rain: build platform or use overhang - In snow: build platform of green logs **Extinguishing:** 1. Separate burning materials 2. Drown with water 3. Stir ashes and add more water 4. Feel with back of hand to ensure cool 5. Cover with dirt **WARNING:** Forest fires can start from seemingly dead coals. Always ensure fire is completely out before leaving. ## Fire in Survival A f

metadata.title: Fire Starting Techniques
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: fires can start from seemingly dead coals. Always ensure fire is completely out before leaving. ## Fire in Survival A fire can save your life through warmth and water purification, but it can also attract unwanted attention or start a wildfire. Always weigh the risks versus benefits based on your specific situation. **Priority Fire Uses:** 1. Hypothermia treatment (life-threatening) 2. Water purification (critical within 72 hours) 3. Signaling rescue 4. Cooking and morale 5. Predator deterrent Master at least two fire-starting methods — one primitive, one modern. Practice before you need them.

metadata.title: Fire Starting Techniques
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: # Emergency Shelter Building *Original content based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles (public domain)* ## Shelter Priority Shelter is often your first priority in survival situations. Exposure to elements kills faster than hunger or thirst. A good shelter maintains body temperature and protects from wind, rain, snow, and sun. ## Site Selection **Ideal Location:** - Dry ground, slightly elevated to avoid water pooling - Natural windbreaks (rocks, dense vegetation) - Near water source but above flood line - Avoid valley bottoms (cold air sinks) - Access to building materials - Visible to rescuers if desired **Avoid:** - Dry riverbeds (flash flood risk) - Directly under dead trees or loose rocks - Animal trails or dens - Low spots that collect water - Avalanche or rockfall zones - Insect nests (check trees and ground) ## Insulation Principles **Heat Loss Prevention:** - Ground draw

metadata.title: Emergency Shelter Building
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: rockfall zones - Insect nests (check trees and ground) ## Insulation Principles **Heat Loss Prevention:** - Ground draws heat faster than air — insulate underneath first - Layer materials for air pockets (better insulation) - Smaller enclosed spaces retain heat better - Block wind completely - Keep shelter entrance at 90° angle to prevailing wind **Insulation Materials:** - Pine needles, leaves, moss, grass - Bark pieces - Snow (excellent insulator when compacted) - Evergreen boughs - Any dry, dead vegetation **Rule of Thumb:** Insulation should be at least 6 inches thick on all sides, 12+ inches in extreme cold. ## Shelter Types ### 1. Lean-To Shelter **Best Use:** Short-term, mild weather, when you have limited time. **Construction:** 1. Find or place sturdy horizontal ridge pole between two trees (waist to chest height) 2. Lean branches at 45° angle against ridge pole 3. Layer smalle

metadata.title: Emergency Shelter Building
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: al ridge pole between two trees (waist to chest height) 2. Lean branches at 45° angle against ridge pole 3. Layer smaller branches and vegetation over frame 4. Add thick layer of leaves, grass, or bark for waterproofing 5. Build up from bottom to top (like shingles) 6. Insulate floor with thick bed of dry vegetation **Advantage:** Quick to build, easy to add fire reflector wall opposite opening. **Disadvantage:** Only protects from one direction, less heat retention. ### 2. Debris Hut **Best Use:** Cold weather, limited materials, solo survival. **Construction:** 1. Create ridgepole from long branch, one end on ground, other supported 3-4 feet high 2. Place ribs (branches) on both sides at 45° angle 3. Fill framework with debris (leaves, grass, pine needles) 2-3 feet thick 4. Cover with layer of sticks to hold debris in place 5. Stuff interior completely with dry insulation material 6. C

metadata.title: Emergency Shelter Building
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: ick 4. Cover with layer of sticks to hold debris in place 5. Stuff interior completely with dry insulation material 6. Create door plug from vegetation **Interior:** Should be just big enough to fit your body, not larger. Small space = retained body heat. **Advantage:** Excellent insulation, uses only natural materials, warm in extreme cold. **Disadvantage:** Time-consuming, requires large amount of debris. ### 3. Snow Cave **Best Use:** Deep snow conditions, extreme cold, winter mountaineering. **Construction:** 1. Find snowdrift or slope with packed snow at least 6 feet deep 2. Dig entrance tunnel upward into drift 3. Hollow out sleeping chamber above tunnel entrance level 4. Chamber ceiling should be domed (stronger, prevents drips) 5. Smooth interior ceiling to prevent dripping 6. Poke small ventilation hole through roof with stick 7. Mark exterior with branches so you can find it **

metadata.title: Emergency Shelter Building
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: ent dripping 6. Poke small ventilation hole through roof with stick 7. Mark exterior with branches so you can find it **Critical:** Sleeping chamber must be higher than entrance (cold air sinks). Keep ventilation hole clear. **Temperature:** Inside temperature typically stays near 32°F regardless of outside temperature (can be -40°F outside). **WARNING:** Always carry digging tool. Never seal entrance completely. Carbon dioxide buildup can be fatal. ### 4. Poncho/Tarp Shelter **Best Use:** When you have manufactured materials, need quick setup. **A-Frame Configuration:** 1. Tie cordage between two trees at chest height 2. Drape poncho/tarp over line 3. Stake out corners at 45° angle 4. Weight edges with rocks or logs **Enhancements:** - Dig shallow drainage ditch around perimeter - Add insulation layer inside - Use additional poncho for ground cloth **Advantage:** Fast, waterproof, light

metadata.title: Emergency Shelter Building
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: perimeter - Add insulation layer inside - Use additional poncho for ground cloth **Advantage:** Fast, waterproof, lightweight materials. **Disadvantage:** Requires manufactured materials. ### 5. Tree Pit Shelter (Winter) **Best Use:** Deep snow, evergreen forest. **Construction:** 1. Find large evergreen tree with low-hanging branches 2. Dig down to ground around trunk (snow is shallow near trunk) 3. Use removed snow to build up walls 4. Lay evergreen boughs on floor and over top opening 5. Leave small opening for entry and ventilation **Advantage:** Natural insulation from tree and snow, quick to build. ## Shelter Improvements **Fire Reflector Wall:** - Build stone or log wall opposite shelter opening - Reflects heat back into shelter - Position fire 3-4 feet from entrance **Drainage:** - Dig shallow trench around shelter perimeter - Prevent water from running inside during rain **Bedd

metadata.title: Emergency Shelter Building
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: rance **Drainage:** - Dig shallow trench around shelter perimeter - Prevent water from running inside during rain **Bedding:** - Never sleep directly on ground - Build raised bed platform if possible - Thick insulation layer critical (test by lying down — if ground feels cold through insulation, add more) ## Emergency Considerations **Time Available:** - Less than 1 hour: find natural shelter (cave, rock overhang, fallen tree) - 1-3 hours: build basic lean-to - 3+ hours: build debris hut or snow cave **Physiological State:** - If hypothermic: get out of wind immediately, any shelter better than none - If injured: prioritize easy construction over optimal insulation A mediocre shelter built before dark is better than a perfect shelter half-finished. Start early, build quickly, improve over time.

metadata.title: Emergency Shelter Building
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: # First Aid Basics *Original content based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles (public domain)* ## Survival First Aid Priorities In survival situations, medical care focuses on preventing life-threatening conditions and stabilizing injuries until rescue. Know your limits — some injuries require professional medical care. ## Assessment: ABC Method Always check in this order: 1. **Airway** — Is it clear? Can they breathe? 2. **Breathing** — Are they breathing adequately? 3. **Circulation** — Is there severe bleeding? Do they have a pulse? **Don't move injured person unless:** - Immediate danger (fire, flooding, combat zone) - Need to perform CPR (must be on firm, flat surface) - Location prevents proper treatment ## Severe Bleeding Control **Life-threatening bleeding kills in minutes.** This is your highest medical priority. ### Direct Pressure **Procedure:** 1. Expose wound (cut awa

metadata.title: First Aid Basics
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: g kills in minutes.** This is your highest medical priority. ### Direct Pressure **Procedure:** 1. Expose wound (cut away clothing if needed) 2. Apply sterile dressing or cleanest cloth available 3. Press firmly directly on wound 4. Maintain pressure for 5-10 minutes minimum 5. If blood soaks through, add more dressings on top (don't remove first one) 6. Bandage firmly in place once bleeding stops ### Pressure Points If direct pressure fails, apply pressure to artery supplying that limb: - **Arm:** Brachial artery (inside of upper arm) - **Leg:** Femoral artery (groin area) Press artery against bone while maintaining direct pressure on wound. ### Tourniquet (Last Resort) **Only use for:** - Severe limb bleeding not controlled by pressure - Multiple casualties (allows you to treat others) - Amputation or partial amputation **Procedure:** 1. Place tourniquet 2-3 inches above wound, never o

metadata.title: First Aid Basics
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: u to treat others) - Amputation or partial amputation **Procedure:** 1. Place tourniquet 2-3 inches above wound, never on joint 2. Tighten until bleeding stops completely 3. Mark time applied on tourniquet (use blood if necessary) 4. Do NOT loosen or remove once applied 5. Seek immediate medical evacuation **WARNING:** Tourniquets can result in limb loss. Only use when death from bleeding is imminent. ## Wound Care **Cleaning:** 1. Wash hands or use cleanest water available 2. Irrigate wound with clean/purified water 3. Remove visible debris gently 4. Pat dry with clean cloth **Dressing:** 1. Apply antibiotic ointment if available 2. Cover with sterile dressing 3. Bandage firmly but not so tight it cuts circulation 4. Check circulation below wound (color, warmth, sensation) 5. Change dressing daily and inspect for infection **Infection Signs:** - Increasing redness spreading from wound -

metadata.title: First Aid Basics
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: ion) 5. Change dressing daily and inspect for infection **Infection Signs:** - Increasing redness spreading from wound - Swelling - Heat - Pus or foul odor - Red streaks running from wound - Fever **Action:** Keep wound clean and dry. If infected, increase dressing changes and seek medical help urgently. ## Fractures and Splinting **Fracture Indicators:** - Severe pain at injury site - Deformity or unnatural angle - Inability to use limb - Swelling and bruising - Grating sensation (crepitus) ### Splinting Procedure **Principles:** - Immobilize joint above and below fracture - Pad splint material (prevent pressure sores) - Check circulation before and after splinting - If limb is blue or without pulse, gently try to align — this is life-over-limb situation **Improvised Splints:** - Straight sticks or branches - Rolled newspapers or magazines - Cardboard - Internal splinting (tape injured

metadata.title: First Aid Basics
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: plints:** - Straight sticks or branches - Rolled newspapers or magazines - Cardboard - Internal splinting (tape injured finger to adjacent finger) **Application:** 1. Position limb as close to natural position as tolerated 2. Place padded splints on both sides of fracture 3. Tie above and below fracture, not on fracture site 4. Check circulation (color, warmth, can they feel touch?) ## CPR Basics **When to Perform:** - Person not breathing - No pulse **Adult CPR:** 1. Call for help / prepare for evacuation 2. Place person on firm, flat surface 3. Position hands center of chest (lower half of breastbone) 4. Push hard and fast: 2 inches deep, 100-120 compressions per minute 5. After 30 compressions, give 2 rescue breaths (if trained) 6. Continue 30:2 ratio until person breathes or help arrives **Compression-Only CPR:** If untrained in rescue breathing, continuous chest compressions alone s

metadata.title: First Aid Basics
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: eathes or help arrives **Compression-Only CPR:** If untrained in rescue breathing, continuous chest compressions alone still saves lives. **Don't stop** until: - Person starts breathing - Professional help arrives - You're too exhausted to continue - Scene becomes unsafe ## Shock Treatment **Shock Signs:** - Pale, cool, clammy skin - Rapid, weak pulse - Rapid breathing - Anxiety, confusion - Weakness or dizziness **Treatment:** 1. Treat underlying cause (stop bleeding, etc.) 2. Lay person flat (or position for comfort if chest injury) 3. Elevate legs 12 inches (if no spinal injury) 4. Maintain body temperature (prevent heat loss) 5. Loosen restrictive clothing 6. Do NOT give food or drink 7. Reassure and keep calm ## Heat Injuries ### Heat Exhaustion **Signs:** Heavy sweating, weakness, nausea, headache, pale/clammy skin. **Treatment:** 1. Move to shade 2. Remove excess clothing 3. Cool

metadata.title: First Aid Basics
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: eating, weakness, nausea, headache, pale/clammy skin. **Treatment:** 1. Move to shade 2. Remove excess clothing 3. Cool with water (drink if conscious, wet skin if not) 4. Rest with legs elevated ### Heat Stroke (Life-Threatening) **Signs:** High body temperature, red/hot/dry skin, rapid pulse, confusion, unconsciousness. **Treatment:** 1. Emergency evacuation needed immediately 2. Move to shade 3. Remove clothing 4. Cool aggressively (water, fanning, ice packs on neck/armpits/groin) 5. Continue cooling until body temperature drops ## Cold Injuries ### Hypothermia **Mild Signs:** Shivering, confusion, fumbling hands, slurred speech. **Severe Signs:** No shivering, rigid muscles, barely conscious, weak pulse. **Treatment:** 1. Get out of cold/wet environment 2. Remove wet clothing 3. Insulate from ground 4. Wrap in dry blankets/sleeping bag 5. Apply heat sources (fire, warm water bottles

metadata.title: First Aid Basics
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: t clothing 3. Insulate from ground 4. Wrap in dry blankets/sleeping bag 5. Apply heat sources (fire, warm water bottles to core — chest, neck, groin) 6. Give warm, sweet drinks if conscious 7. Do NOT rub skin or give alcohol **WARNING:** Severe hypothermia victims seem dead but may be revived. Handle gently, rewarm slowly. ### Frostbite **Signs:** White or grayish-yellow skin, numbness, hard/waxy skin texture. **Treatment:** 1. Do NOT thaw if there's risk of refreezing (causes severe damage) 2. Remove wet/constrictive clothing 3. If safe to thaw: immerse in warm water (98-102°F / 37-39°C) 4. Do NOT rub or massage 5. Separate affected digits with gauze 6. Protect from further cold ## Dehydration **Signs:** - Dark urine or decreased urination - Dry mouth and lips - Dizziness - Rapid heartbeat - Sunken eyes **Treatment:** - Drink water slowly and steadily - Add pinch of salt and sugar to ai

metadata.title: First Aid Basics
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: ess - Rapid heartbeat - Sunken eyes **Treatment:** - Drink water slowly and steadily - Add pinch of salt and sugar to aid absorption - Rest in shade - If severe, medical evacuation needed ## Survival First Aid Kit Essentials **Minimum:** - Sterile gauze pads - Adhesive bandages (various sizes) - Elastic bandage - Trauma dressing - Antiseptic wipes - Antibiotic ointment - Pain relievers - Tourniquet - Medical tape - Scissors/knife **Remember:** Knowledge is more important than equipment. You can improvise materials, but you cannot improvise knowledge. ## Legal and Ethical Considerations **Good Samaritan Principle:** In emergencies, render aid to the best of your ability. Document what you do and when. **Limits:** Know when an injury exceeds your capability. Stabilize and evacuate rather than attempt procedures beyond your training.

metadata.title: First Aid Basics
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: # Navigation and Orientation *Original content based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles (public domain)* ## The Importance of Navigation Getting lost can turn a minor survival situation into a life-threatening emergency. Understanding basic navigation prevents wasted energy, dehydration, and panic. ## Map Reading Basics ### Topographic Maps **Contour Lines:** Show elevation changes. - Lines close together = steep terrain - Lines far apart = gentle slope - Lines forming a V pointing uphill = valley/drainage - Lines forming a V pointing downhill = ridge **Map Legend:** Identifies symbols for roads, water, buildings, vegetation. **Scale:** Determines distance (e.g., 1:24,000 means 1 inch = 24,000 inches on ground). **Declination Diagram:** Shows difference between true north and magnetic north (critical for accurate compass use). ### Orient Your Map 1. Align map's north with compass

metadata.title: Navigation and Orientation
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: rue north and magnetic north (critical for accurate compass use). ### Orient Your Map 1. Align map's north with compass north 2. Adjust for magnetic declination 3. Match terrain features (hills, water) to map symbols 4. Identify your location by triangulating known landmarks ## Compass Use ### Parts of a Compass - **Magnetic Needle:** Always points magnetic north - **Rotating Bezel:** Marks degrees (0-360°) - **Direction-of-Travel Arrow:** Where you're heading - **Orienting Lines:** Align with north on map ### Taking a Bearing 1. Point direction-of-travel arrow at landmark 2. Rotate bezel until orienting arrow aligns with magnetic needle 3. Read bearing number at index line 4. Follow direction-of-travel arrow, keeping needle aligned ### Following a Bearing 1. Set bearing on bezel 2. Hold compass flat at waist level 3. Rotate your body until magnetic needle aligns with orienting arrow 4.

metadata.title: Navigation and Orientation
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: g on bezel 2. Hold compass flat at waist level 3. Rotate your body until magnetic needle aligns with orienting arrow 4. Walk toward direction-of-travel arrow 5. Pick intermediate landmark and walk to it 6. Repeat until destination reached **Common Errors:** - Metal objects nearby (belt buckles, knives) interfere with compass - Holding compass at wrong angle - Not adjusting for magnetic declination ## Natural Navigation (No Equipment) ### Sun Navigation **Northern Hemisphere:** - Sun rises in east, sets in west (approximately) - At noon, sun is due south - Shadows point north at solar noon **Shadow Stick Method:** 1. Place stick vertically in ground 2. Mark tip of shadow with stone 3. Wait 15-30 minutes 4. Mark new shadow tip 5. Line between marks runs east-west (first mark is west) 6. Perpendicular line runs north-south ### Star Navigation **Northern Hemisphere - Polaris (North Star):**

metadata.title: Navigation and Orientation
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: ark is west) 6. Perpendicular line runs north-south ### Star Navigation **Northern Hemisphere - Polaris (North Star):** 1. Find Big Dipper constellation 2. Locate two stars forming outer edge of "cup" 3. Draw imaginary line through these stars 4. Extend line about 5 times the distance 5. Bright star you reach is Polaris = true north **Southern Hemisphere - Southern Cross:** 1. Find Southern Cross constellation 2. Draw imaginary line along long axis 3. Extend line 4.5 times its length 4. Drop line down to horizon = south ### Natural Indicators (Less Reliable) **Moss Growth:** Common myth that moss only grows on north side is FALSE. Moss grows on the dampest side, which varies by region. **Tree Growth:** Trees often (but not always) have fuller branches on south side (in Northern Hemisphere) due to more sunlight. **Ant Hills:** In northern regions, often have steeper slope on north side. *

metadata.title: Navigation and Orientation
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: Northern Hemisphere) due to more sunlight. **Ant Hills:** In northern regions, often have steeper slope on north side. **Use these only as secondary indicators, never as primary navigation.** ## Dead Reckoning When visibility is poor or terrain featureless: 1. **Set Direction:** Choose bearing using compass or natural methods 2. **Pace Count:** Determine how many steps you take per 100 meters (count every time left foot strikes) 3. **Track Distance:** Count paces as you travel 4. **Record:** Note direction and distance traveled at each turn 5. **Plot:** Keep mental or written map of your route **Practice your pace count:** Varies with terrain, load, fatigue. Test on known distance. ## Landmark Navigation **Handrails:** Linear features to follow (rivers, roads, ridgelines, fences). **Baselines:** Large features you can't miss (coastline, major highway, mountain range). **Catching Features

metadata.title: Navigation and Orientation
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: s, fences). **Baselines:** Large features you can't miss (coastline, major highway, mountain range). **Catching Features:** Landmark beyond destination that signals you've gone too far. **Attack Point:** Obvious landmark near destination you navigate to first. **Example:** To find small cabin (destination), first navigate to river (attack point), then follow river (handrail) to cabin. If you reach bridge (catching feature), you've gone too far. ## Staying Found **Prevention:** - Note prominent landmarks continuously - Look back frequently (terrain looks different in reverse) - Estimate travel speed and time - Stay oriented to major terrain features - If uncertain, STOP — don't compound the problem **If Lost:** 1. **STOP** — Sit down, stay calm 2. **THINK** — Where did you last know your location? 3. **OBSERVE** — What landmarks are visible? 4. **PLAN** — Best course of action (stay put v

metadata.title: Navigation and Orientation
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: ou last know your location? 3. **OBSERVE** — What landmarks are visible? 4. **PLAN** — Best course of action (stay put vs. navigate out) ## When to Stay Put vs. Move **Stay Put If:** - People know where you are and expect your return - You're injured or exhausted - Weather is poor - Night is falling - You're completely disoriented **Move If:** - No one knows your location - You can see clear route to safety (road, town, river to follow) - Staying puts you in danger (avalanche zone, flood area) - You have map and compass and know where you are ## Urban and Developed Area Navigation - Follow power lines (lead to civilization) - Follow streams downhill (lead to larger water, often settlements) - Head toward sounds of traffic, trains, or aircraft patterns - Look for light glow on night horizon (indicates towns) **Remember:** In survival navigation, approximate direction toward safety is bett

metadata.title: Navigation and Orientation
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: low on night horizon (indicates towns) **Remember:** In survival navigation, approximate direction toward safety is better than precise direction to nowhere. Priority is getting out alive, not arriving at exact GPS coordinates.

metadata.title: Navigation and Orientation
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: # Food Foraging and Procurement *Original content based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles (public domain)* ## Food in Survival Situations The human body can survive weeks without food but only days without water. **Food is rarely your first priority** — shelter, water, and signaling typically come first. However, prolonged survival situations require caloric intake. **Energy Economics:** Foraging burns calories. Don't expend more energy obtaining food than the food provides. ## Universal Edibility Test **CRITICAL:** Only use when you cannot identify a plant with certainty and no other food is available. This test takes 24+ hours. **Never test:** - Mushrooms or fungi (too dangerous) - Plants with milky or discolored sap - Plants with beans, bulbs, or seeds inside pods - Plants with thorns, fine hairs, or spines - Plants with dill, carrot, parsnip, or parsley-like foliage (many are

metadata.title: Food Foraging and Procurement
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: pods - Plants with thorns, fine hairs, or spines - Plants with dill, carrot, parsnip, or parsley-like foliage (many are poisonous) - Plants with three-leaved growth pattern (potential poison ivy) **Procedure:** 1. **Separate:** Test only one part of plant at a time (leaf, stem, root) 2. **Smell:** Crush and smell. Discard if strong or acid odor 3. **Skin Contact:** Rub plant juice on inside of elbow. Wait 15 minutes. If burning, itching, or rash appears, discard 4. **Lip Test:** Place small amount on outer lip. Wait 3 minutes. Discard if burning or irritation 5. **Tongue Test:** Place on tongue, hold for 15 minutes. Don't swallow. Discard if burning or irritation 6. **Chew Test:** Chew small amount for 15 minutes. Don't swallow. Discard if burning or irritation 7. **Swallow Test:** Swallow the chewed portion. Wait 8 hours. Eat and drink nothing else during this time 8. **Monitor:** If n

metadata.title: Food Foraging and Procurement
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: allow Test:** Swallow the chewed portion. Wait 8 hours. Eat and drink nothing else during this time 8. **Monitor:** If nausea, vomiting, cramps, or diarrhea occur, induce vomiting and drink plenty of water. Plant is not edible 9. **Eating Test:** If no ill effects, eat 1/4 cup of same plant part. Wait 8 hours 10. **Confirmation:** If no ill effects, plant part is safe to eat **Cook all wild plants when possible** — cooking destroys some toxins. ## Edible Plant Categories (General Principles) **WARNING:** Proper plant identification requires detailed knowledge and ideally field guides with pictures. These are general categories only. ### Generally Safer Categories **Berries:** - Blue/black berries = usually safe - White/yellow berries = usually poisonous - Red berries = 50/50 — many safe, many poisonous - **Always test unknown berries using edibility test** **Nuts and Acorns:** - Most nut

metadata.title: Food Foraging and Procurement
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: 50/50 — many safe, many poisonous - **Always test unknown berries using edibility test** **Nuts and Acorns:** - Most nuts are edible - Acorns require leaching (soak in water, change water until not bitter) **Grasses:** - Most grass seeds are edible when cooked - Avoid ergot (black/purple fungus on grain heads — poisonous) **Roots and Tubers:** - Many are edible when cooked - Must positively identify or use edibility test ### Common Edible Plants (Wide Geographic Distribution) **Dandelion:** Entire plant edible (leaves, root, flowers). Young leaves less bitter. **Cattails:** Roots, shoots, and pollen edible. Young shoots taste like cucumber. **Clover:** Entire plant edible. Flowers and seeds most nutritious. **Plantain:** Leaves edible raw or cooked (not banana plantain — the common lawn weed). **Pine:** Inner bark (cambium layer) edible. Pine needle tea high in vitamin C. **CRITICAL:** A

metadata.title: Food Foraging and Procurement
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: — the common lawn weed). **Pine:** Inner bark (cambium layer) edible. Pine needle tea high in vitamin C. **CRITICAL:** Always use reliable field guides for positive identification. These examples are for educational purposes — misidentification can be fatal. ## Trapping and Hunting ### Simple Snare **Principle:** Create loop that tightens when animal passes through. **Materials:** - Wire, cord, or strong plant fiber - Anchor (stake or fixed object) **Placement:** - Game trails (look for tracks, droppings, fur) - Burrow entrances - Fence crossings **Legal Note:** Check local regulations. Snaring may be illegal except in survival emergencies. ### Figure-4 Deadfall Trap **Concept:** Weight (rock/log) held up by trigger, falls when animal disturbs bait. **Components:** - Vertical stick (upright) - Diagonal stick (connects vertical to horizontal) - Horizontal stick (holds bait) - Heavy weight

metadata.title: Food Foraging and Procurement
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: rtical stick (upright) - Diagonal stick (connects vertical to horizontal) - Horizontal stick (holds bait) - Heavy weight **Set on small game trails.** Practice construction before emergency. ## Fishing Without Gear **Improvised Hooks:** - Carved bone or wood shaped into hook - Thorns from plants - Safety pins bent into hooks **Gorge Hook:** - Small sharp stick, sharpened at both ends - Tie line to middle - Hide in bait - When fish swallows, stick turns sideways and lodges **Spearing:** - Use straight sapling, sharpen end or split into multiple prongs - Fish in shallow water - Aim low (water refracts light, fish is deeper than appears) **Trapping:** - Build rock/stick funnel in stream (wide mouth narrows to trap) - Check tidal pools after tide goes out ## Insects and Invertebrates **High protein, easy to catch, abundant.** **Generally Safe:** - Grasshoppers/crickets (remove wings and legs

metadata.title: Food Foraging and Procurement
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: ertebrates **High protein, easy to catch, abundant.** **Generally Safe:** - Grasshoppers/crickets (remove wings and legs) - Grubs and larvae (found in rotting logs) - Earthworms (purge in water for 24 hours) - Ants (most species edible, can be sour) **Avoid:** - Brightly colored insects (often poisonous) - Insects with fine hairs - Disease-carrying insects (flies, mosquitoes, ticks) - Spiders, scorpions, centipedes **Preparation:** Cook when possible. Remove wings, legs, stingers. ## Food Preservation **Drying (Jerky):** 1. Cut meat into thin strips 2. Hang in sun or near fire (not in smoke) 3. Dry until hard and brittle 4. Store in dry location **Smoking:** 1. Build slow, cool smoke fire (no flame) 2. Hang meat above smoke 3. Smoke for 24-48 hours **Cold Storage:** - Bury in snow - Submerge in cold stream (secure in bag) - Hang in shade, away from scavengers ## Foraging Safety Rules 1.

metadata.title: Food Foraging and Procurement
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: ury in snow - Submerge in cold stream (secure in bag) - Hang in shade, away from scavengers ## Foraging Safety Rules 1. **Never eat unknown plants** without testing or expert knowledge 2. **Avoid mushrooms entirely** unless you're an expert 3. **Cook everything possible** 4. **Start with small amounts** even with "safe" foods 5. **Note what you eat** so if you get sick, you know cause 6. **Don't eat moldy, slimy, or foul-smelling food** (desperation is not worth death) **Remember:** You can survive weeks without food. Don't risk poisoning yourself for a single meal. When in doubt, don't eat it.

metadata.title: Food Foraging and Procurement
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: # Signaling and Rescue *Original content based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles (public domain)* ## Signaling Principles Being found requires being seen or heard. Effective signaling can cut days off your survival ordeal. **Key Principles:** - **Contrast:** Signal must stand out from environment - **Movement:** Attracts attention better than static displays - **Repetition:** Signal continuously or at intervals - **Location:** Signal from highest, most visible point safe to reach - **Preparation:** Have signals ready before you see/hear rescue ## Visual Signals ### Signal Fire **Three fires in triangle pattern** = international distress signal (each fire 25-30 meters apart). **Day Signals (Smoke):** - Add green vegetation, rubber, or oil to create dark smoke - White smoke shows well against dark forest - Black smoke shows well against snow or light terrain - Keep fire smoldering,

metadata.title: Signaling and Rescue
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: hite smoke shows well against dark forest - Black smoke shows well against snow or light terrain - Keep fire smoldering, not flaming **Night Signals (Flame):** - Build fire on high point visible from distance - Keep flames bright and large - Three fires still more recognizable than one **Maintenance:** - Keep fuel ready to add when aircraft heard - Have tinder ready to restart if needed - Position fires in clearing, not under trees ### Signal Mirror **Most effective daytime signal** — visible for miles. **Procedure:** 1. Hold mirror near face 2. Extend other hand toward target (aircraft, boat, distant rescuer) 3. Reflect sunlight onto extended hand 4. Adjust mirror angle to "bounce" light from hand to target 5. Flash repeatedly (SOS pattern if possible) **Improvised Mirrors:** - Any reflective surface (CD, phone screen, belt buckle, polished can bottom) - Even non-perfect reflection can

metadata.title: Signaling and Rescue
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: rors:** - Any reflective surface (CD, phone screen, belt buckle, polished can bottom) - Even non-perfect reflection can be visible from miles away ### Ground-to-Air Signals **Create large symbols visible from aircraft:** **SOS** — Universal distress (three short, three long, three short) **Other Signals:** - **X** = Need medical assistance - **F** = Need food and water - **II** = Need medical supplies - **→** = Proceeding in this direction **Construction:** - Each line 3-6 meters wide, 12+ meters long - Use contrasting materials (rocks on snow, logs on grass, cleared areas in vegetation) - Position in open area visible from multiple directions ### Panel Signals **Orange or bright colored material:** - Wave clothing or fabric - Lay panels in X or SOS pattern on ground - Attach to poles and wave **VS-17 Panel** (military signal panel): - Orange on one side, pink/violet on other - Highly vi

metadata.title: Signaling and Rescue
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: Attach to poles and wave **VS-17 Panel** (military signal panel): - Orange on one side, pink/violet on other - Highly visible against most backgrounds ## Audio Signals ### Whistle **Advantages:** Carries farther than voice, requires less energy, doesn't damage throat. **Pattern:** - Three blasts = universal distress - Repeat at intervals **Range:** Up to 1 mile in open terrain, less in forest. ### Shouting **Effective but exhausting:** - Cup hands around mouth - Shout toward likely rescuer direction - Three shouts, pause, repeat **Preserve voice** — use whistle when possible. ### Noise Makers - Bang metal objects together - Strike hollow logs - Use any available sound-producing method ## Electronic Signals ### Cell Phone **Even without service:** - Keep battery charged - Turn off when not signaling to conserve power - Move to high ground to check signal - Send text messages (require less

metadata.title: Signaling and Rescue
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: - Turn off when not signaling to conserve power - Move to high ground to check signal - Send text messages (require less signal than calls) - Use phone flashlight for signaling at night (SOS pattern) ### Personal Locator Beacon (PLB) / Emergency Position Indicating Radio Beacon (EPIRB) **If you have one:** - Activate only in genuine emergency - Transmits location to rescue satellites - Keep dry and protect from damage - Don't turn off once activated until rescue arrives ## SOS Pattern **International Distress Signal:** Three short, three long, three short (· · · — — — · · ·) **Applications:** - Whistle blasts - Light flashes - Sound signals - Ground patterns **Morse Code SOS timing:** - Short = 1 count - Long = 3 counts - Pause between signals = 1 count - Pause between letters = 3 counts ## Day vs. Night Strategies **Daytime:** - Signal mirror (most effective) - Smoke signals - Ground pa

metadata.title: Signaling and Rescue
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: letters = 3 counts ## Day vs. Night Strategies **Daytime:** - Signal mirror (most effective) - Smoke signals - Ground panels - Waving bright materials **Nighttime:** - Fire (bright flames) - Flashlight or improvised torch - Reflections from fire on mirrors/reflective surfaces - Chemical light sticks if available ## Signaling Aircraft **When you hear aircraft:** 1. **Move to clearing immediately** 2. **Activate signal** (mirror, fire, panel) 3. **Wave bright materials** with large motions 4. **Stand in open** where you're visible 5. **Continue signaling** until aircraft clearly acknowledges or passes **Aircraft Acknowledgment:** - Rocking wings = message received - Circling = you've been spotted - Green flare/light = proceed as signaled - Red flare/light = await further signals ## Body Signals to Helicopters **Spread eagle (Y shape):** YES — need pickup **Arms down in V shape:** NO — don'

metadata.title: Signaling and Rescue
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: signals ## Body Signals to Helicopters **Spread eagle (Y shape):** YES — need pickup **Arms down in V shape:** NO — don't need pickup **Both arms raised straight up:** Pick up here **One arm up, one down (diagonal):** Need medical assistance **Waving with both arms:** Can proceed on own, don't need assistance ## Signaling Strategy **Priority:** 1. Stay visible and in open areas when possible 2. Keep signals ready to activate instantly 3. Signal continuously once rescuers are in area 4. Have backup signals prepared **Remember:** Rescue depends on being found. Invest energy in effective signaling. One successful signal can save days or weeks of survival struggle.

metadata.title: Signaling and Rescue
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: # Emergency Medical Situations *Original content based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles (public domain)* ## Life-Threatening Medical Emergencies Some injuries and medical conditions require immediate action. In survival situations, quick recognition and treatment can prevent death. ## Allergic Reactions / Anaphylaxis **Severe allergic reactions can kill in minutes.** ### Signs and Symptoms **Mild to Moderate:** - Hives, itching, rash - Swelling of lips, tongue, or face - Nausea or vomiting **Severe (Anaphylaxis):** - Difficulty breathing, wheezing - Swelling of throat/tongue blocking airway - Rapid, weak pulse - Dizziness or loss of consciousness - Blue lips or fingernails ### Treatment **If epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) available:** 1. Remove from packaging 2. Hold firmly against outer thigh (can inject through clothing) 3. Press and hold for 3 seconds 4. Massage injection

metadata.title: Emergency Medical Situations
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: ng 2. Hold firmly against outer thigh (can inject through clothing) 3. Press and hold for 3 seconds 4. Massage injection area for 10 seconds 5. Keep person lying down 6. Second dose may be needed after 5-15 minutes if no improvement **Without epinephrine:** 1. Help person sit in position easiest for breathing 2. If person stops breathing, begin CPR 3. Evacuate immediately — this is life-threatening **Prevention:** If known allergy, avoid triggers. Carry epinephrine if prescribed. ## Snake and Spider Bites ### Venomous Snake Bite **Signs:** - Puncture marks (one or two) - Immediate severe pain and burning - Swelling spreading from bite - Nausea, vomiting - Difficulty breathing - Blurred vision **Treatment:** 1. Get away from snake (avoid second bite) 2. Keep person calm and still (movement spreads venom) 3. Remove jewelry/tight clothing before swelling starts 4. Position bite below heart

metadata.title: Emergency Medical Situations
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: and still (movement spreads venom) 3. Remove jewelry/tight clothing before swelling starts 4. Position bite below heart level if possible 5. Clean wound gently 6. Immobilize bitten limb with splint 7. Mark edge of swelling with pen, note time 8. Evacuate to medical care immediately **DO NOT:** - Cut the bite or try to suck venom - Apply ice or tourniquet - Give alcohol or stimulants - Waste time trying to catch/kill snake **Note:** Most snake bites are not fatal if treated properly. Stay calm, immobilize, evacuate. ### Spider Bites (Black Widow, Brown Recluse) **Black Widow Signs:** - Immediate sharp pain - Muscle cramps and rigidity - Nausea, vomiting - Difficulty breathing **Brown Recluse Signs:** - Initially painless - After hours: blister forms at bite site - Tissue death (necrosis) spreading from bite - Fever, chills, nausea **Treatment:** 1. Clean wound 2. Apply cool compress 3. El

metadata.title: Emergency Medical Situations
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: death (necrosis) spreading from bite - Fever, chills, nausea **Treatment:** 1. Clean wound 2. Apply cool compress 3. Elevate bite area 4. Give pain relievers if available 5. Monitor for worsening symptoms 6. Seek medical care (antivenom exists for black widow) ## Broken Bones (Fractures) ### Open Fracture (Bone Through Skin) **Extreme emergency** — risk of infection and severe bleeding. **Treatment:** 1. Control bleeding (direct pressure on wound, not on bone) 2. Do NOT push bone back in 3. Cover wound with sterile dressing 4. Immobilize with splint as is 5. Treat for shock 6. Evacuate immediately ### Closed Fracture **Treatment:** 1. Immobilize injured area 2. Splint above and below fracture 3. Check circulation before and after splinting 4. Apply ice if available (reduce swelling) 5. Elevate if possible 6. Give pain medication if available **Circulation Check:** - Press fingernail or

metadata.title: Emergency Medical Situations
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: duce swelling) 5. Elevate if possible 6. Give pain medication if available **Circulation Check:** - Press fingernail or toe (turns white) - Release and watch for color return (should return within 2 seconds) - Check warmth and sensation ## Hypothermia **Body temperature drops below 95°F / 35°C.** ### Progressive Symptoms **Mild (95-90°F):** - Shivering (intense) - Confusion, difficulty speaking - Fumbling hands, stumbling - Drowsiness **Moderate (90-82°F):** - Violent shivering, then stops - Irrational behavior, paradoxical undressing - Muscle rigidity - Slow, shallow breathing - Weak pulse **Severe (below 82°F):** - Unconsciousness - Barely detectable pulse/breathing - Rigid muscles - Appears dead ### Treatment **Mild Hypothermia:** 1. Get out of cold/wet environment 2. Remove wet clothing 3. Insulate from ground 4. Wrap in dry blankets/sleeping bags 5. Give warm, sweet drinks (if consc

metadata.title: Emergency Medical Situations
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: . Remove wet clothing 3. Insulate from ground 4. Wrap in dry blankets/sleeping bags 5. Give warm, sweet drinks (if conscious and able to swallow) 6. Skin-to-skin contact in sleeping bag (rescuer and victim) **Severe Hypothermia:** 1. Handle extremely gently (rough handling can cause cardiac arrest) 2. Don't assume person is dead (they may be revivable) 3. Insulate from further heat loss 4. Rewarm core only (chest, neck, groin) — not extremities 5. If breathing/pulse absent, begin CPR and continue until warm 6. Evacuate with extreme care **DO NOT:** - Rub or massage skin - Give alcohol - Warm too quickly (causes shock) - Apply heat to arms/legs (can cause heart failure) **Critical Rule:** "Nobody is dead until they're warm and dead." ## Heat Stroke **Life-threatening. Body temperature above 104°F / 40°C.** ### Signs and Symptoms - High body temperature - Hot, red, dry skin (or heavy sweat

metadata.title: Emergency Medical Situations
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: dy temperature above 104°F / 40°C.** ### Signs and Symptoms - High body temperature - Hot, red, dry skin (or heavy sweating) - Rapid, strong pulse - Confusion, altered mental state - Seizures - Unconsciousness ### Treatment **This is a medical emergency:** 1. Move to shade/cool environment immediately 2. Remove excess clothing 3. Cool aggressively: - Spray/pour cool water on skin - Fan to increase evaporation - Ice packs on neck, armpits, groin - Immerse in cool water if available 4. Continue cooling until body temperature drops to 102°F / 39°C 5. If conscious, give cool water to drink slowly 6. Monitor breathing and pulse 7. Evacuate immediately **DO NOT:** - Give alcohol or caffeine - Give salt tablets - Use ice water (too cold, can cause shock) ## Drowning / Near-Drowning ### Response 1. Ensure scene safety (don't become second victim) 2. Remove from water 3. Check breathing and pulse

metadata.title: Emergency Medical Situations
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: wning ### Response 1. Ensure scene safety (don't become second victim) 2. Remove from water 3. Check breathing and pulse 4. If not breathing, begin CPR immediately (don't wait to drain water) 5. Continue CPR until breathing resumes or professional help arrives 6. Keep warm (hypothermia common in drowning) 7. Seek medical care even if person recovers (secondary drowning possible hours later) **Note:** People can drown in as little as 2 inches of water. Unconscious persons face-down in any water depth are drowning emergencies. ## Severe Bleeding (Arterial) **Arterial bleeding is pulsing, bright red, and rapidly fatal.** ### Treatment Priority 1. Direct pressure on wound 2. Elevate wounded limb above heart 3. Pressure point (artery against bone upstream from wound) 4. Tourniquet (if limb and bleeding cannot be controlled otherwise) **See First Aid Basics document for detailed bleeding contr

metadata.title: Emergency Medical Situations
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles

chunks.text: niquet (if limb and bleeding cannot be controlled otherwise) **See First Aid Basics document for detailed bleeding control procedures.** ## When to Evacuate Immediately - Altered mental status or unconsciousness - Difficulty breathing - Severe bleeding not controlled - Suspected spinal injury - Open fractures - Chest or abdominal injuries - Anaphylaxis - Heat stroke or severe hypothermia - Severe burns - Venomous bites **Remember:** Survival medicine is about preventing death and stabilizing until professional care is available. Know your limits and evacuate when injuries exceed your capability to treat.

metadata.title: Emergency Medical Situations
metadata.source: Original - based on US Army FM 21-76/FM 3-05.70 principles
